planting.note: Direct-sow seeds on the soil surface in partial shade after frost, or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; jewels of opar self-seeds prolifically in warm climates and, once introduced, typically maintains itself as a self-renewing annual or short-lived perennial groundcover in partial-shade gardens without further sowing.

directions: Direct-sow seeds on soil surface in partial shade or full sun after frost; jewels of opar (Brazilian spinach, fameflower, pink baby's breath) is a tropical perennial succulent of the purslane family (Portulacaceae) that serves a dual role as an edible leafy vegetable and an ornamental plant — the fleshy, oval, bright-green leaves are eaten raw in salads (with a mildly astringent, spinach-like flavor) or cooked as a pot herb, while the plant itself produces an ornamental display of hundreds of tiny, rosy-pink to magenta, five-petaled flowers on widely branched, airy, 12–24-inch flowering stems, creating a delicate pink-mist effect above the succulent foliage. In warm climates it grows as a drought-tolerant, shade-tolerant annual or short-lived perennial that self-seeds freely to maintain permanent colonies in partially shaded garden beds, pathways, and edges. Unlike most leafy greens, it thrives in the combination of heat, drought, and partial shade that defeats most other edibles in Gulf Coast and tropical summers. An excellent dual-purpose plant for edible/ornamental shade gardens in zone 8–12.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in cool spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Good King Henry is a long-lived perennial once established — allow it to remain in place for several years for maximum leaf production, as the crown grows progressively larger and more productive with each passing year.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in cool spring or fall in moist, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Good King Henry is a European perennial edible plant of the goosefoot family (Chenopodiaceae, now Amaranthaceae) that was widely cultivated as a potherb and vegetable in medieval and early modern Europe but has been largely replaced by spinach and other modern leafy vegetables — leaving it as a largely forgotten but highly useful perennial edible for the shade garden. The large, arrow-shaped, dark-green leaves are produced abundantly from an expanding perennial crown that persists for many years; in spring, the young leaves and shoot tips are eaten raw or cooked (with a mild, spinach-like flavor), and the young flowering shoots are eaten like asparagus when they first emerge, blanched and steamed. The flowers themselves are used as a condiment. The primary advantage over annual spinach is permanence — once established, Good King Henry returns year after year with progressively larger harvests from an increasingly robust perennial crown, requiring virtually no replanting. It performs acceptably in partial shade, making it one of the few perennial vegetables useful in the shade vegetable garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide thick rhizomes in spring or fall, or direct-sow seeds in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, poor to average, gritty or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; roseroot thrives in alpine and coastal conditions with cool summers and requires excellent drainage — it fails and rots in hot, humid, poorly drained soil common in zone 7 and below.

directions: Divide rhizomes or direct-sow in well-drained, poor to average, rocky or gritty soil in full sun to partial shade; roseroot (golden root, Arctic rose) is a circumpolar arctic and alpine succulent perennial of the stonecrop family (Crassulaceae) that is one of the most extensively studied adaptogenic herbs in modern phytotherapy — the thick, fragrant rhizome (smelling of roses when cut, hence the name) contains rosavins and salidroside, compounds that have been demonstrated in clinical trials to reduce fatigue, improve physical and mental performance under stress, and support mood in states of mild anxiety and depression. It has been used in Scandinavian, Siberian, and Tibetan traditional medicine for at least 3,000 years as a stamina- and stress-resistance herb, and modern Scandinavian military and sports research has documented its adaptogenic effects. The plant itself is an attractive dwarf succulent with fleshy, toothed, blue-green leaves and clusters of small, bright-yellow to yellow-green, star-shaped flowers in late spring. It is extremely cold-hardy (surviving to zone 1) but requires cool summers and excellent drainage; it fails in warm, humid, poorly drained conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem cuttings, leaf cuttings, or divide clumps in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor, dry soil in full sun to partial shade; orpine is extremely drought-tolerant once established and declines in rich, moist, poorly drained soil — lean, dry conditions produce compact, floriferous plants.

directions: Root cuttings or divide clumps in well-drained, average to poor, dry soil in full sun to partial shade; orpine (live-forever, stonecrop) is a European edible succulent perennial and one of the oldest known medicinal plants, used for wound healing throughout northern Europe since ancient times. Now correctly classified as Hylotelephium telephium, it produces attractive fleshy, blue-green, oval, toothed succulent leaves (edible raw in salads with a mild, slightly acidic flavor, or cooked) on upright stems, topped in late summer and fall by large, flat-headed clusters of star-shaped flowers in shades of deep-rose-pink, crimson-red, or white. The late-summer flowering season (August–October) makes it particularly valuable as a late-season nectar source for monarch butterflies, bumblebees, and migrating painted ladies. Extremely drought-tolerant and long-lived. It is the parent of the Hylotelephium 'Herbstfreude' (Autumn Joy) hybrid — one of the most widely planted autumn-flowering perennials in temperate gardens. 'Matrona' (purple-flushed foliage, dark stems, rose-pink flowers) and 'Purple Emperor' (deep-purple foliage) are outstanding cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist soil or water, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade after frost; mukunuwenna roots readily from stem cuttings placed in moist soil or water and can be harvested repeatedly by tip-pruning, which also promotes bushy new growth for subsequent harvests.

directions: Root stem cuttings or direct-sow in moist, average to fertile soil in partial shade; mukunuwenna (sessile joyweed, dwarf copperleaf, alternanthera) is a pantropical low-growing perennial herb widely consumed as a leafy vegetable throughout South and Southeast Asia — the young shoots and leaves are a staple green vegetable in Sri Lanka (where it is a major ingredient in mallung, a coconut-based herb salad), and in Indian, Thai, and Vietnamese cooking. The mild, slightly crunchy leaves are eaten raw in salads or cooked briefly in stir-fries, curries, and soups. The plant is exceptionally shade-tolerant for a warm-season leafy green, maintaining vigorous production in partial shade where spinach and lettuce would bolt in summer heat, making it a highly practical leafy green for Gulf Coast and tropical summer shade gardens. It grows along the margins of streams, ponds, and wet ditches in its natural habitat and tolerates wet, waterlogged soil conditions. Multiple harvests per season are obtained by tip-pruning. In zone 9–12 it persists as a perennial; elsewhere it is grown as a summer annual.
